skill.name: threat-mitigation-mapping
skill.description: Map identified threats to appropriate security controls and mitigations. Use when prioritizing security investments, creating remediation plans, or validating control effectiveness.
skill.summary: Map threats to security controls for effective risk treatment planning and defense-in-depth strategies.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 54 patterns as potential security issues, but all findings are false positives. The skill consists of markdown documentation files with Python code examples for security planning. Detected patterns include markdown code block formatting (mistaken for shell backticks), documentation reference URLs, and security terminology in example code. No executable malicious code, network calls, or dangerous operations exist.

content.user_title: Map threats to security controls and mitigations
content.value_statement: Security teams struggle to connect identified threats with appropriate countermeasures. This skill provides structured frameworks and control libraries to map threats to effective mitigations.
actual_capabilities: Map threats to preventive, detective, and corrective security controls
Generate mitigation plans with defense-in-depth analysis
Identify control gaps and coverage weaknesses
Recommend controls from a standard security library
Calculate risk reduction and implementation roadmaps
Validate control effectiveness through testing frameworks
limitations: Provides planning and documentation guidance only, does not implement actual security controls
Requires existing threat identification as input
Control recommendations are generic and may need customization for specific environments
Does not replace professional security assessment or penetration testing

use_cases.title: Security Architecture Review
use_cases.description: Review existing security architecture and identify gaps in threat coverage. Map current controls to known threats and prioritize remediation efforts.
use_cases.target_user: Security architects designing or reviewing system security posture

use_cases.title: Risk Treatment Planning
use_cases.description: Create structured remediation roadmaps for identified risks. Prioritize controls based on threat impact, implementation cost, and effectiveness.
use_cases.target_user: Risk managers and security teams planning security investments

use_cases.title: Control Validation
use_cases.description: Validate that existing security controls provide adequate coverage for identified threats. Check for defense-in-depth and control diversity.
use_cases.target_user: Compliance officers and security engineers validating control effectiveness

prompt_templates.title: Basic Threat Mapping
prompt_templates.prompt: I have identified the following threats in my system: [list threats]. Map each threat to appropriate security controls using the preventive, detective, and corrective framework.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting threat mitigation planning with a list of identified threats

prompt_templates.title: Control Gap Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze our current security controls: [list controls]. Identify which threats are not adequately covered and recommend additional controls to close the gaps.
prompt_templates.scenario: Reviewing existing security posture to find coverage gaps

prompt_templates.title: Defense-in-Depth Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my security architecture for defense-in-depth. Current controls are: [list controls at each layer]. Identify missing layers and recommend controls to achieve layered defense.
prompt_templates.scenario: Ensuring multiple security layers protect critical assets

prompt_templates.title: Remediation Roadmap
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prioritized implementation roadmap for these security controls: [list controls]. Consider threat impact, implementation cost, and dependencies. Organize into phases with clear priorities.
prompt_templates.scenario: Planning phased security improvement initiatives with budget constraints

output_examples.input: Map SQL Injection threat to controls
output: - VAL-001: Input Validation Framework (Preventive, Application layer) - Validate and sanitize all user input using parameterized queries
- VAL-002: Web Application Firewall (Preventive, Network layer) - Deploy WAF with SQL injection detection rules
- LOG-001: Security Event Logging (Detective, Application layer) - Log suspicious query patterns for monitoring
Application Layer: ✓ MFA implemented, ✓ Rate limiting active
Data Layer: ✓ Credential encryption at rest
- No detective controls for authentication anomalies
- No corrective controls for compromised accounts
Recommendations: Add login anomaly detection and automated account lockout procedures

best_practices: Map every identified threat to at least one control from each category: preventive, detective, and corrective
Implement controls at multiple layers to achieve defense-in-depth rather than relying on single points of protection
Regularly review and test control effectiveness as threats and systems evolve over time
anti_patterns: Relying on a single control type such as only preventive measures without detection or response capabilities
Implementing controls without measuring their effectiveness or verifying they work as intended
Treating security controls as set-and-forget without regular review and updates as the threat landscape changes

faq.question: What is defense-in-depth and why does it matter?
faq.answer: Defense-in-depth means implementing security controls at multiple layers such as network, application, and data. If one control fails, other layers still provide protection. This reduces single points of failure in your security architecture.

faq.question: How do I prioritize which controls to implement first?
faq.answer: Prioritize controls that address critical and high-impact threats first. Consider implementation cost, effectiveness, and whether the control mitigates multiple threats. Quick wins with high effectiveness and low cost should be prioritized.

faq.question: What is the difference between preventive, detective, and corrective controls?
faq.answer: Preventive controls stop attacks before they occur like firewalls or input validation. Detective controls identify attacks in progress like intrusion detection systems. Corrective controls respond to and recover from attacks like incident response procedures.

faq.question: How often should I review my threat mitigation mappings?
faq.answer: Review threat mappings at least annually or when significant changes occur such as new features, infrastructure changes, or emerging threats. Security controls can degrade in effectiveness over time and need regular validation.

faq.question: Can this skill help with compliance requirements?
faq.answer: Yes. The control library includes references to common compliance frameworks like PCI-DSS, NIST, and SOC2. Mappings can demonstrate due diligence in addressing identified risks which supports compliance audits.

faq.question: What if I have limited security budget?
faq.answer: Focus on high-effectiveness, low-cost controls first. Input validation, logging, and access controls typically provide strong ROI. The skill can optimize recommendations based on budget constraints and help build a phased implementation roadmap.
